content.text: Control Framework Mapping for Claude Programs

An enterprise Claude program must satisfy requirements from multiple overlapping frameworks simultaneously — NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, GDPR, SOC 2, and increasingly the EU AI Act. Without a formal mapping between the controls you have implemented and the requirements of each framework, compliance evidence is produced ad hoc, coverage gaps are invisible until an audit reveals them, and the same evidence must be assembled multiple times for different assessors. A control library with framework mappings eliminates this duplication.

attrs.alt: Control framework mapping: Claude enterprise control library mapped bidirectionally to six external frameworks

content.text: The control library is the single source of truth. Each control has a stable identifier, a description, and a set of framework mappings. When an auditor requests evidence for GDPR Article 25 (data protection by design), the mapping tells you exactly which Claude controls satisfy that requirement and where the evidence lives — no manual research required.

The table below shows a representative subset of the Claude control library with mappings to six frameworks. A full production library would cover approximately 45 controls spanning data, model, operational, legal, and reputational risk categories.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal
from datetime import date

ControlCategory = Literal["data", "model", "operational", "legal", "reputational"]
EvidenceType = Literal["automated_metric", "configuration", "log", "policy_document", "attestation"]

@dataclass
class FrameworkMapping:
    framework: str          # e.g. "NIST_AI_RMF", "ISO_42001", "GDPR", "SOC2", "EU_AI_ACT"
    requirement_id: str     # e.g. "MAP 1.5", "A.7.2", "Art. 25"
    requirement_title: str  # human-readable name of the framework requirement

@dataclass
class Control:
    control_id: str                  # e.g. "CTRL-DATA-001"
    title: str
    description: str
    category: ControlCategory
    implementation_status: Literal["implemented", "partial", "planned", "not_applicable"]
    evidence_type: EvidenceType
    evidence_location: str           # path, URL, or system reference where evidence lives
    owner: str
    review_frequency_days: int       # how often effectiveness must be verified
    last_reviewed: date
    framework_mappings: list[FrameworkMapping] = field(default_factory=list)

    def frameworks_covered(self) -> set[str]:
        return {m.framework for m in self.framework_mappings}

    def is_overdue_review(self) -> bool:
        from datetime import date as d
        delta = (d.today() - self.last_reviewed).days
        return delta > self.review_frequency_days
Loading and Querying the Control Library

When preparing for an audit — SOC 2 renewal, ISO 42001 certification, GDPR DPA review — generate a framework-specific evidence package that lists every requirement, the control(s) that satisfy it, and the location of the evidence.

The 'gaps' field in the evidence package is the deliverable that matters most to auditors. A gap is a framework requirement for which no implemented control provides evidence. Generating this list automatically ensures gaps are surfaced during preparation rather than discovered during the audit itself.

Run a gap analysis before each major audit cycle to identify requirements that are not yet covered by any implemented control. Map gaps back to open risk register entries so the remediation work is tracked in the same governance system.

# Define the requirements for each framework we must satisfy
FRAMEWORK_REQUIREMENTS = {
    "GDPR": [
        ("Art. 5(1)(f)", "Integrity and confidentiality"),
        ("Art. 7", "Conditions for consent"),
        ("Art. 13", "Information to be provided"),
        ("Art. 25", "Data protection by design"),
        ("Art. 44", "General principle for transfers"),
    ],
    "NIST_AI_RMF": [
        ("GOVERN 1.2", "AI risk governance policies"),
        ("MAP 1.5", "AI risk identification"),
        ("MEASURE 2.5", "AI risk measurement"),
        ("MANAGE 1.3", "AI risk response"),
        ("MANAGE 2.2", "Adversarial input treatment"),
    ],
}

def gap_analysis(controls: list[Control], framework: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Return requirements with no implemented control.
    """
    reqs = FRAMEWORK_REQUIREMENTS.get(framework, [])
    gaps = []
    for req_id, req_title in reqs:
        satisfying = controls_for_requirement(controls, framework, req_id)
        implemented = [c for c in satisfying if c.implementation_status == "implemented"]
        if not implemented:
            gaps.append({
                "requirement_id": req_id,
                "requirement_title": req_title,
                "partial_controls": [c.control_id for c in satisfying if c.implementation_status == "partial"],
                "planned_controls": [c.control_id for c in satisfying if c.implementation_status == "planned"],
            })
    return gaps

Controls evolve: a new regulatory requirement may tighten an existing control, a technology change may make a control obsolete, or an audit finding may require a control to be redesigned. Treat the control library like application code — version it, test it, and require review before changes are merged.

# control_library.yaml header — version tracked in git
# Each breaking change (new requirement, evidence type change) increments minor version.
# Non-breaking additions (new framework mappings) increment patch.

"""
library_version: "1.4.2"
last_updated: "2026-06-15"
approved_by: "Head of Compliance"
changes:
  - version: "1.4.2"
    date: "2026-06-15"
    description: "Added EU AI Act Art. 14 mapping to CTRL-OPS-002"
  - version: "1.4.1"
    date: "2026-04-01"
    description: "CTRL-DATA-003 evidence location updated to new consent store DB path"
  - version: "1.4.0"
    date: "2026-01-10"
    description: "Added CTRL-MOD-003: bias monitoring for HR-adjacent Claude features"
controls:
  - control_id: CTRL-DATA-001
    ...
"""

Store the library under governance/control_library.yaml in the same repository as the risk register. A CI check that runs gap_analysis() on every pull request prevents merges that would introduce uncovered framework requirements without a corresponding new or updated control.

Some controls can be verified automatically on a schedule rather than waiting for a quarterly manual review. Automated control monitoring produces an evidence stream that is always current, reducing audit preparation time from weeks to hours.
Run scan on a 1% random sample of RAG corpus chunks
Yes — any undetected PII in sample
Check for API invocations without matching consent record
Compare actual token spend to configured quota
Automated test set evaluation via RAG ground truth

from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
import json

CONTROL_MONITOR_LOG = Path("/var/data/risk/control_monitor.jsonl")

def log_control_check(
    control_id: str,
    passed: bool,
    metric_value,
    threshold,
    details: str = "",
):
    record = {
        "ts": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "control_id": control_id,
        "passed": passed,
        "metric_value": metric_value,
        "threshold": threshold,
        "details": details,
    }
    with open(CONTROL_MONITOR_LOG, "a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")
    if not passed:
        alert_control_failure(control_id, metric_value, threshold, details)

def alert_control_failure(control_id, metric_value, threshold, details):
    print(f"CONTROL FAILURE: {control_id} | value={metric_value} threshold={threshold} | {details}")

Automated control monitoring turns compliance from a periodic snapshot into a continuous stream of evidence. When an auditor asks for evidence that CTRL-DATA-002 (output redaction) was operating effectively during Q3, the monitor log provides timestamped pass/fail records for every hour of the quarter — not just a summary from the last manual review.

A control framework mapping library eliminates the duplication of compliance evidence assembly by assigning each Claude enterprise control a stable identifier and a set of cross-framework requirement mappings. The library is stored as versioned YAML, loaded programmatically for coverage reporting and gap analysis, and used to generate framework-specific audit evidence packages that list every requirement, the controls that satisfy it, and where the evidence lives. Gap analysis run before each audit cycle surfaces uncovered requirements early enough for remediation rather than discovery during the audit itself.
